@tacaswell I restructured the `bandpass`, `scale_to_gamut` and `measure_noise` APIs.

In short, the new `scalefactor_to_gamut` calculates the necessary scalefactor. `scale_to_gamut` optionally takes the scalefactor, if not provided it calls `scalefactor_to_gamut`.

`bandpass` is back to the original state, with only some cosmetic changes

`measure_noise` now optionally takes the bandpassed image as argument. If not provided, it will call bandpass by itself. So if we provide the bandpassed image in `locate`, we circumvent a second call to the (slow) bandpass function and probably increase performance when `characterize=True`


Still pending is a test function to actually test the static error calculation. I will push it soon. It is absolutely necessary to put a `threshold` when noise size is high. Possibly a global otsu could be built into `measure_noise`, and used if threshold is not given.

I ran into this issue when trying to link some particles into trajectories. I have stretches where the particles disappear from the frame for 10 frames or more which is much greater than my memory setting (memory=3). I fixed it by changing [linking.py](https://github.com/soft-matter/trackpy/blob/master/trackpy/linking.py#L1090). From the current master:
```python
if self.predictor is not None:
    # This only works for KDTree right now, because KDTree can store particle
    # positions in a separate data structure from the PointND instances.
    if not isinstance(prev_hash, TreeFinder):
         raise NotImplementedError(
                        'Prediction works with the "KDTree" neighbor_strategy only.')
    # Get the time of cur_level from its first particle
    t_next = list(itertools.islice(cur_level, 0, 1))[0].t
    targeted_predictor = functools.partial(self.predictor, t_next)
    prev_hash.rebuild(coord_map=targeted_predictor) # Rewrite positions
```

In empty frames `cur_level` is an empty list. So instead I use the `prev_level` and add one to it. It seems to work for me. As a band aid I changed it with a `try: except:` in order to keep the original code intact as possible. Here is what it looks like in my local version:
```python
            if self.predictor is not None:
                # This only works for KDTree right now, because KDTree can store particle
                # positions in a separate data structure from the PointND instances.
                if not isinstance(prev_hash, TreeFinder):
                    raise NotImplementedError(
                        'Prediction works with the "KDTree" neighbor_strategy only.')
                # Get the time of cur_level from its first particle
                try:
                    t_next = list(itertools.islice(cur_level, 0, 1))[0].t
                except IndexError:
                    t_next = list(itertools.islice(prev_level, 0, 1))[0].t + 1
                targeted_predictor = functools.partial(self.predictor, t_next)
                prev_hash.rebuild(coord_map=targeted_predictor) # Rewrite positions
```

It seems to work fine for my purposes perhaps because the length of time that the particles spend out of frame is longer than my current memory setting (which is fine with me).

Also this might be a naive question but I don't understand the point of using `list(itertools.islice(cur_level, 0, 1))[0].t` anyway. If `cur_level` is just a list of `Point` objects and you want the first one why not just use `cur_level[0].t`?

@kalbasit the nature of the problem that https://github.com/deis/deis/commit/5878b8f8c3d7d625351d93589cf8223d06a93bbb fixes is that even though the duplicate controller doesn't show up in (for example) `deisctl list | grep controller`, it may in fact still be running. You can check for this with something along the lines of the following:

```sh
for ip in $(fleetctl list-machines | grep -v IP | awk '{print $2}'); do
  ssh $ip 'docker ps' | grep controller;
done
```
Unfortunately you may have other components besides the controller running as well, so you might want to replace the `| grep controller` above with `|grep deis` to check for those.

Want to add one more note to make clear what my goals are here, in case it wasn't before.  The three things I think we need to optimize for, in order, are:

1. decompression speed
2. memory footprint during decompression (preferably tunable, e.g. at compression time)
3. compressed artifact size

I'm willing to give up a LOT of compression speed for the benefit of the other three.

I think ideally we'd be able to balance priorities 1 & 2 depending on platform target.

At this point I don't have enough information to have a favorite algorithm.  I'm not partial to any at this point.  I'm also not scared of the newer algorithms with less exposure and battle testing, if they bring with them compelling improvements.  We already have bzip2 for the traditionalists.

If what we want is for `conda` to completely _disregard_ all packages in lower-priority channels, we can do that, but that has never been the designed behavior, and that is not what is documented, either.

I just found a pretty serious issue with this branch. Underscores are not handled correctly. This means that all the packages in the R channel are not being ordered right. A basic example is:

```
In [6]: VersionOrder('0.17_3') > VersionOrder('0.17')
Out[6]: False
```

We have to use underscores in the r channel because the R packages use dashes, but dashes are not allowed in conda versions.  An underscore should be treated the same as a `.` for ordering purposes. 

Also, in the future, can you be more careful about removing trailing whitespace from your files before committing?

Yeah exactly. It would be worth pointing out hyphened options in the style guide in the development manual: https://github.com/Clinical-Genomics/development/issues/56

That being said, TB is older than `cg` and the options are named less consistently (e.g. `--log-level` but `--dryrun`).

I guess eugeniodilo requested a function to do the intend/un-intend by "n" colums without the need to change "n" in the settings (easily accessed by [F2]). But I'm with Colin and say intend/un-intend is enough.

An option to switch between free and fixed form is really nice although the only things for switching fixed to free are:

* if there's content after column 72 (while this is a missing setting in OCIDE as cobc allows changing this - which may be necessary if you want to work with COBOL sources written for other vendors) and it's not already part of an inline comment: prefix it with ``*>``
* if ``-`` is found in column 7: go further until a line does not start with ``*`` or ``-`` and do the following backwards:
    * if the next item start with a ``'`` or ``"`` add the ``-`` at the end of the previous line after the last ``'`` or ``"`` (skip any comments)
    * if not it's part of a statement, place the next word directly at the end of the previous line [without a space between!] (skip any comments)
* replace ``*`` in column 7 with ``*>``
* replace ``\`` in column 7 with ``*>\`` (won't do the same as there won't be a line break in listing, but necessary for later change free-fixed
* replace ``D`` in column 7 with ``>>D`` (only valid for GnuCOBOL as this is a non-common extension)
* optional: un-intend by 6

An automatic way of combining lines is not useful as the programmer likely has added line breaks in most cases because there should be line breaks.

For free-to fixed: everything listed before in switched order but additional check if a resulting source text would exceeds column 72, if yes: place this source text to the next line with the same intend.

Really not necessary but nice-to-have :-)
Maybe waiting for an HackEdit plugin is a reasonable option.

No, it’s not the same tests. Every time will load new configuration. In the python script will generate new set of parameters and launch VUnit tests.

The use case for external libraries is for rarely changed 3rd party dependencies such as the unisim library that are compiled outside of VUnit and which do not need to be incrementally compiled or have dependency tracked by VUnit. In this situation in general a change of the external library always requires a --clean compile within VUnit since the specific dependencies between the VUnit managed files and the external library files are not know. 

In the specific case such as yours one could argue that a change of the external library should not require a recompile of a VUnit tracked file but that is not generally true and depends on code and simulator specifics. In your case GHDL considers the test bench architecture dependent on the entity definition of axis_fifo and I believe other simulators would agree.

@erosen03 Do the following steps (assuming your container name is `vhds`):
1. stop your vm
2. run `az storage blob copy start --source-blob <source blob name> --source-container vhds -b <your desired copy name> -c vhds --account-name <storage account name>`
3. get your copied blob url using `az storage blob url -c vhds --account-name <> -n <blob copy name>` or portal
4. run `az vm update --set storageProfile.osDisk.vhd.uri=<copy url> -g <group name> -n <vm name>`
5. `az vm show` should show you an updated os disk

I was able to run these steps with no problems.

My most typical rsync command line is
`rsync -av [--delete] /original /destination`

The idea is to make the destination directory tree a mirror copy of the origin.   Support that and you've probably met the 80/20 requirement.  

Mostly i don't checksum files - the default checks size+mtime+name and that is typically good enough.  However, it would be a nice feature to support in a second round.  Example usage:  A fixity check of an online backup.  Not sure how you'd pull that off without retrieving the object from storage.  If you could modify the storage backend to actually compute a checksum real-time and return it, that'd be interesting - but that's a much bigger ask.  Simply comparing the local file's checksum to the checksum you stored when you stored the object is interesting - much more so if the platform already does that fixity check on each object as part of a regular "scrub" cycle.  

I don't care much about the rsync algorithm for sending diffs of large files, although I can imagine it could be very useful in some cases - e.g., incrementally updating a vm disk image backup or a large database dump file.  

Bonus features that plain rsync doesn't support would be local encryption and compression before you upload the object.

[docs.microsoft.com/cli/azure/batch/node/user#create](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/batch/node/user#create)

The Linux user config on the pool that requires the private key is for a different scenario where the Batch service impersonates / runs a work load as a specified user. This concept is discussed further in this article:
[docs.microsoft.com/azure/batch/batch-user-accounts](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/batch/batch-user-accounts)

Additionally, the specific scenario that requires the private ssh key is documented in the Batch API:

`The SSH private key is used to automatically configure password-less SSH between nodes in a Linux pool when the pool's enableInterNodeCommunication property is true. If not specified, password-less SSH is not configured between nodes.`

oh I bet I know what's happening here!

Here's how I can demonstrate your exact reproduction:

## Step 1: add and commit these

### .pre-commit-config.yaml

```yaml
repos:
-   repo: https://github.com/chewse/pre-commit-mirrors-pydocstyle
    rev: v2.1.1 
    hooks:
    - id: pydocstyle
```

### setup.cfg

```ini
[pydocstyle]
ignore=
```

### mymod.py

```python
"""A very good docstring."""
```

## Step 2: Modify the contents to look like this *but do not stage them*

### setup.cfg

```ini
[pydocstyle]
ignore=D100
```

### mymod.py

```python
def f():
    pass
```

If you run `git diff` it should look like this:

```diff
diff --git a/mymod.py b/mymod.py
index 0c8cd5a..4f28bc7 100644
--- a/mymod.py
+++ b/mymod.py
@@ -1 +1,2 @@
-"""A very good docstring."""
+def f():
+    pass
diff --git a/setup.cfg b/setup.cfg
index d88917d..56565bf 100644
--- a/setup.cfg
+++ b/setup.cfg
@@ -1,2 +1,2 @@
 [pydocstyle]
-ignore=
+ignore=D100,D103
```

## Step 3: commit just `mymod.py`

```console
$ git commit mymod.py
[WARNING] Unstaged files detected.
[INFO] Stashing unstaged files to /home/asottile/.cache/pre-commit/patch1535122114.
Verifying PEP257 Compliance..............................................Failed
hookid: pydocstyle

mymod.py:1 at module level:
        D100: Missing docstring in public module
mymod.py:1 in public function `f`:
        D103: Missing docstring in public function

[INFO] Restored changes from /home/asottile/.cache/pre-commit/patch1535122114.
```

This _is_ working as intended, here's what's happening:

1. `git commit mymod.py` will *only* commit `mymod.py` and not other files in the working directory
2. `pre-commit` knows this, and so it sets the state of the checkout to that of what's being committed.  In this case, it temporarily undoes the changes to `setup.cfg` (you can verify this by peeking at th epatch file it creates):

    ```diff
    $ cat /home/asottile/.cache/pre-commit/patch1535122114
    diff --git a/setup.cfg b/setup.cfg
    index d88917d..56565bf 100644
    --- a/setup.cfg
    +++ b/setup.cfg
    @@ -1,2 +1,2 @@
     [pydocstyle]
    -ignore=
    +ignore=D100,D103
    ```
3. then the commit fails!

If that commit were to succeed (which many other frameworks get wrong) you'd have a commit which _fails_ linting being committed! (which is kind of the point of `pre-commit` to prevent!).  In your case, make sure to `git commit setup.cfg path/to/file.py` and you should be good to go :)

Note that the actual `git` hook just invokes `pre-commit run` (usually with no arguments) and not `pre-commit run --files ...`.  `pre-commit run --files ...` is a convenience helper for commandline usage and avoids stashing.

## bonus Step 4: commit both files at once

```console
$ git commit -m 'change!' mymod.py setup.cfg 
Verifying PEP257 Compliance..............................................Passed
[master f38958b] change!
 2 files changed, 3 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
```

> the purpose of objects implementing the mapping interface is so people can write functions that work on dict's, and if you happen to have an instance instead, you can use the same function.

Fair enough! The trouble is, right now the `keys()` etc. methods are not safe to use. They're not even used by Schematics itself because they would fail on any model that has a field with an overlapping name.

So, if the methods are going to stay, then I think we should
* explicitly forbid any field names that would cause a conflict
* accurately define the iterators' semantics; e.g., should serializables be included.

> In my days of building Brubeck (the web framework), the difference between using objects or functions for routing turned out to be roughly 33% gains when using just functions.

You mean a scenario involving Schematics? If you still have the testcase, I'd love to get it for benchmarking.

Accessing plain dictionaries is obviously faster, but in the context of Schematics this only accounts for a fraction of the CPU costs. And any such performance difference could be optimized away by making the transformation loops aware of instances and having them access `_data` directly.

When importing, the resulting (internal) data structure is a newly-populated dictionary in any case. So literally the only difference between returning a dictionary vs. a model instance is the creation of the model object, which doesn't seem very expensive in the grand scheme of things.

Hence, it's my belief that, if there are any notable performance gaps between plain dictionaries and model instances, they are merely design issues that can be fixed.

One scenario where, historically, there has been a significant performance penalty is basic validation. Passing a dict to `validate()` is efficient because both conversion and validation are performed at once:

```python
validate(MyModel, inputdict)
```

Doing the same thing using an instance involves importing first and validating after:

```python
MyModel(inputdict).validate()
```

Since validation also has the conversion step baked in, the whole import process is run twice for no reason.

This is addressed in PR #374, which improves the performance of the above scenario by ~40%. The functional variant is now only about 3% faster than creating and validating instances.

> Btw, Kalle - you're doing a goddam great job with all your Schematics work. I have only been speaking up when I have something different from you to say, which means you don't hear from me too often.

I don't want to make too drastic changes if neither users nor maintainers take a stance one way or another, so it's important to know people are at least watching what goes on.

Many ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files do simply download and compile/uncompress binaries and move them to ``$INSTALL_DIR``. So perhaps it would make sense to ``<dep_dir>/<name>/<version>/bin/`` as an option for where the bash script should put things?

This issue is a multi-headed hydra, so I'll try to address each aspect in segments:

**Expiration is uniquely perilous in a system without restorative overrides.**

Because IDs cannot be forcibly taken in this system (good), they are also ~permanently lost once acquired by another entity (potentially very bad). The vision is that this system's IDs will come to serve as the most prominent and long-lasting form if identifier/identity anchor the user ever owns. Because of this the stakes are very high.

**Signing to IDs, not keys, is a blessing and a curse**

To rid ourselves of the need for attestation and data resigning loops in coordination with the entities the user has accumulated signed payloads from, it is desirable to have attestations and data signed in relation to the ID, not a key. This is great for many parts of the system UX/ops, but puts an even greater burden on retaining control of your ID, as a irrevocable loss of control becomes far more damaging to the individual. 

**I'm always skeptical of subjective thresholds and intervals**

Why is the chronological progression of renewal the right basis, even if we think it could provide some of benefits we want? I understand the inference of essentially testing the user's resolve with timed ownership challenges, then rewarding with extensions, but is that the best we can do? I always like to see systems based on more deterministic mechanisms, checks, or validations, or no renewal at all (unless an ID was voluntarily released).

I can't really provide a solid answer yet about the time strategy, because I need to think for a while on whether there is any more accurate, empirical, objective basis to achieve the goals, or if renewal should be required at all.

_Note: If valuable names cost way more to begin with, it would mitigate some of the need for renewal._

Ok, [here's our working handshake](https://github.com/EUDAT-B2STAGE/python-irodsclient/blob/b5431dc10d907e302659d49b760c3014230b7f2b/irods/connection.py#L171-L189) with the GSS API towards our iRODS server through the GSI plugin.

Two notes:

1. We had to learn how to write messages to iRODS to exchange tokens. That part didn't belong to this issue.
2. To provide a valid client context with GSI mechanism, this is the peace of code that work:
```python
import gssapi

GSI_OID = "1.3.6.1.4.1.3536.1.1" 

gsi_mech = gssapi.raw.OID.from_int_seq(GSI_OID)

server_name = gssapi.Name(SERVER_DN)
server_name.canonicalize(gsi_mech)

client_ctx = gssapi.SecurityContext(name=server_name, mech=gsi_mech, usage='initiate')

```

where `SERVER_DN` is the DN of the server X509 certificate.
You may start it with
```python
SERVER_DN = "/CN=test"
```
and the client should tell you which server_dn is expected.

The GSI mechanism should be configured on the client host, for example like so:
```bash
$ more /etc/gss/mech.d/gsi.conf

# GSI mechanism plugin
#
# copy this file in /etc/gss/mech.d
#
# Mechanism Name    Object Identifier       Shared Library Path         Other Options
gsi_v1          1.3.6.1.4.1.3536.1.1		/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libglobus_gssapi_gsi.so
```

Thanks so much for helping, hope our code will help others too.

As the reset urls can only be used once by virtue of their tokens changing after use, I don't think this is so severe.

It looks like the password reset email sending gets its instruction to use https (or not) from the standard Django auth implementation, which passes on the state of the request that requests the reset, so we don't have that in other contexts; we'd have to duplicate the logic.

My (personal) opinion is that as there's no privilege escalation that can happen here. Protecting `store/password-reset/…` is just part of configuring TLS on your site. It'll only impact people that whitelist URLs inside the store as being HTTPS only *and* include the profile edit page but not the password reset page.

I'd say this is closable, as it's such a minor edge case, compared to the original ticket.

> lifecycle

The main thing we want to avoid is supporting an operating system version that we know will go out of service while a given .NET Core release is in service. For example, if [Ubuntu 16.10](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/Releases) will go out of service 12 months into a .NET Core release, we'd rather not support it from the start. I would propose providing some additional apache arguments might help with supporting many cases out there. I use Gentoo, whose setup for apache is fairly different from Debian-based systems:

* Gentoo has separate apache2ctl and apache2 commands, whereas the letsencrypt-apache plugin seems to want a single command to both manage the service and to query apache's configuration. On Gentoo with apache 2.4, `apache2ctl` is a wrapper script that forwards commands to the init script (it can start, stop, configtest, etc.), while the `apache2` command accepts various flags, such as the "-D DUMP_RUN_CFG" used to get the runtime apache configuration.
* Gentoo makes heavy use of the -D flags for modules.

The existing `--apache-ctl` option does not provide enough flexibility for both of these cases.

* Gentoo uses a single /etc/apache2/vhosts.d directory, instead of having /etc/apache2/sites-enabled and a sites-available.

I don't think there is anything to configure this either, since sites-enabled is hard-coded in several places.

@Casyfill There is currently no "official" way to do this. This is essentially a duplicate of #125.

There are, as far as I can see, two hack-y ways to do this as a workaround in the meantime. Probably the "better" hack-y way to do it (which doesn't rely on the private interface at all) is to run the parser twice with different `default` dates, and make sure that the `day` value doesn't change between them:

```
from dateutil import parser
from datetime import datetime, time

def parse_with_day(dtstr):
    df1 = datetime.combine(datetime.now().date(), time(0, 0)).replace(day=1)
    df2 = df1.replace(day=15)
    
    d1 = parser.parse(dtstr, default=df1)
    d2 = parser.parse(dtstr, default=df2)

    if d1.day == d2.day:
        print("Has date: {}".format(d1))
    else:
        print("No date")

with_day = "March 7, 2008"
sans_day = "March 2008"

parse_with_day(with_day)
parse_with_day(sans_day)
```

The more fragile approach that _doesn't_ involve parsing the same date twice is to subclass `parser` and add a check for the date into the `_parse` method. This is probably more elegant, but relies on the private interface, which may change in future versions:

```python
from dateutil import parser
from datetime import datetime, time

class NoDayPresentError(ValueError):
    pass


class strictparser(parser.parser):
    def _check_result_for_day(self, result):
        day_value = getattr(result, 'day')

        if getattr(result, 'day', None) is None:
            raise NoDayPresentError

    def _parse(self, *args, **kwargs):
        res, skipped_tokens = super(strictparser, self)._parse(*args, **kwargs)

        self._check_result_for_day(res)

        return res, skipped_tokens


DEFAULT_SP = strictparser()

def parse_with_day(dtstr):
    try:
        dt = DEFAULT_SP.parse(dtstr)
        print('Has date: {}'.format(dt))
    except NoDayPresentError:
        print('No day present')

sp = strictparser()

with_day = "March 7, 2008"
sans_day = "March 2008"

parse_with_day(with_day)
parse_with_day(sans_day)
```

I found this was caused by the inaccurate metadata of this transcript (NM_001145051.2) from RefSeq: The last exon should be 499 - 681 rather than 499 - 680. The final A base of the transcript was added to complete the stop codon, but the RefSeq seemed to forget to adjust the last exon metadata accordingly. The SeqRepo uses the RefSeq annotations: ```python >>> tx_identity_info = hdp.get_tx_identity_info('NM_001145051.2') >>> tx_identity_info ['NM_001145051.2', 'NM_001145051.2', 'transcript', 21, 681, [138, 360, 182], 'TMEM247'] >>> tx_identity_info["lengths"] [138, 360, 182] ``` The length of last exon should be 183, rather than 182. This makes the hgvs consider the length of this transcript to be 680, rather than 681. https://github.com/biocommons/hgvs/blob/03cb886524bc368e04cf3be0d0d1372b2109a66d/hgvs/transcriptmapper.py#L78 ```python >>> tgt_len = sum(tx_identity_info["lengths"]) >>> tgt_len 680 ``` This causes the missing of the last A base of this transcript in hgvs.

While you are right that the compilation of the C client probably failed and fell back to the Python client, there is still an issue when installing the Python client. The Python client keeps the relative shebang `#!/usr/bin/env python` (and thus uses the python from the virtualenv instead of the system python). In all the other scripts, the shebang is replaced by the absolute shebang `#!/usr/bin/python` or `#!/usr/bin/python2`.

When `scripts/powerline` is the python client, it should not be added to the `data_files` argument. That way distutils will replace the relative shebang with an absolute one.

Powerline-daemon has no reason to be a C binary, powerline is written in Python and daemon+C client is a workaround for slow startup. Whether or not you are using daemon delay in generation should be I/O in first place, just without daemon you will have to load Python and import lots of modules and with daemon you may only sometimes get problems with VCS repositories, especially in Mac OS where you either have slower modification-time-based watcher for file changes or have to install pyuv (which is known to miss some events).

Arguments to the segment are invalid:

1. `shutdown_event` argument *must not* be supplied. Dunno how it appeared in documentation at all, but even if you wanted to set it to `None` you should’ve used `null`.
2. `update_first` set to true is default, no need to specify it. And it should be boolean, not string. `true` is boolean, `"true"` is string.
3. `format` argument you supplied looks like default… but it is not. Default is `"{0:.0}%"`, *not* `"u'{0:.0f}%'"`. `u'…'` is Python unicode string and here is JSON with different quoting style.
4. `interval` 1 is default, no need to specify it. Though this should not cause any harm.

Errors are mostly harmless, except for `shutdown_event` setting. It looks like you don’t need to supply `args` dictionary at all.

@fijal To make it transparent, which do you prefer?

* By filename extension.  
  (pros: clear, easy to use another compression format.  cons: filename is required for reading data).
* By sniffing starting bytes. (When starting with 00 03 00, it's plain profile data, when starting with 1f 8b 08, it's gzip.)  
  (pros: completely transparent, backward compatible, cons: not generic, hard to support another compression format).

If you're seeing this with numpy, then I suspect the reason is that you're using `np.dot` or `np.linalg` or similar, so it's calling into an optimized BLAS/LAPACK library like OpenBLAS or MKL. And then that library is starting up multiple threads internally to farm out the work over multiple cores. Vmprof doesn't know what to do with these threads, since they're created independently of Python and aren't running Python code. And unfortunately, there's really no way for vmprof (or any profiler) to figure out that when it sees this thread over here using CPU, then it should count that against the `np.dot` call that's happening over in another thread somewhere else :-(.

I've found that running my script with the environment variable `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` can help – this disables threading in most BLAS libraries. Of course, now you're running with threading disabled, so you need to keep that in mind when interpreting your profiling data – it won't be as representative of how you normally run your script when you're not profiling. But the data can still be very useful.

AES-KW is more secure in the sense that it does not require random numbers, so it’s not susceptible to low-quality random number generators. I think that both modes would be secure enough for us. This is my understanding: IGNPAR is only for the receiver. (It does nothing to the transmit side.) It tells the receiver that there is going to be a bit received where the parity bit is, but to ignore it. For example, if you set the receiver for 8N1, and you send a message that has a parity bit (IE: 8O1), you can expect a framing error from most receivers. This way we can receive a message with an unknown parity bit and not create an error. (What is completely missing from the standard as far as I can tell is a way to pass the state of this bit up to the upper layers. Other than a hack of setting !IGNPAR and PARMRK then setting either MARK or SPACE parity and watching for either '\377\0<data>' or '<data>'. This is a mess to decode, especially if the arriving data may include '\377\0'... You may even have to set ISTRIP and bark up that tree. Either way, the standard has issues...

It's not intended, but it happens because of the design.
When a Symbol is created, a Symbol in the C++ is also created and this C++ object is referenced in the Python Symbol.
Arithmetic operations happen on the C++ side and therefore it knows only about the C++ object, not the Python object and when args is called it gives back a new Python object wrapping the C++ object.

One possible solution for this would be to subclass the C++ object and keep a pointer to the python object and return the python object instead of creating a new Python object wrapping the C++ object.
One disadvantage over this is that this creates a cyclic reference and the symbols are never freed.

@chriseth Support for statically disabling components is not implemented but it is definitely do-able. If you're going to give this a go I'd advise you do this in the CMake build system and not the legacy build system because you're going to have an easier time.

If you look at https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/d2e27f6f1f33cf6e0ae05cb78976a35fd6423f5f/src/CMakeLists.txt#L111 you can see that the list of components comes from a global property named `Z3_LIBZ3_COMPONENTS`. This component gets appended to by the `z3_add_component` macro.

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/d2e27f6f1f33cf6e0ae05cb78976a35fd6423f5f/cmake/z3_add_component.cmake#L46-L283

You can use this as basis for statically disabling components. Here is a sketch for how I think you should implement this.

1. Add a new CMake cache option `Z3_ENABLED_COMPONENTS` that is a list of Z3 components to include in `libz3`. This value would be used in the `z3_add_component()` macro and it would not not build that component (and therefore not append to the component to `Z3_LIB_Z3_COMPONENTS`) if the value does not appear in the list. However we will have a special `all` value for this list that means build all components.

2. Teach `z3_add_component()` to define a component specific compiler macro (e.g. `Z3_COMPONENT_NLSAT_ENABLED) when the component is actually enabled and then conditionalize using `#ifdef`s all of Z3's code where those components get loaded so that it is possible to compile Z3 without some components loaded.

This would then allow you as a consumer of Z3 to set `Z3_ENABLED_COMPONENTS` to be whatever you want at configure time.

One thing that will need careful handling is transitive dependencies. You need to decide if it's the user's job to explicitly list transitive dependencies in `Z3_ENABLED_COMPONENTS` or if they should be inferred automatically. If they are to be inferred automatically the implementation will be more complicated.

Change is perfectly fine. The use of the C API in the repro above is potentially very unsafe: the caller does not take a reference count on the objects (AST pointers) returned from Z3. Their life-time is undefined if they are not used immediately after to define a new AST of if a reference count is not acquired. The C++ wrappers handle this automatically so it is much better to use the C++ mode.

Yep, most of the changes should be pretty straightforward. Just a bit tedious at the most :) Rebasing is not too hard, basically:

* check out the most recent master, make sure it's up to date
* then check out the branch you want to rebase
* `git rebase master`
* this will try to rebase everything automatically, but there will likely be merge conflicts. git will then tell you what to do to fix them. Here's how I do this (there might be more efficient ways):
* `git status` to see which files have merge conflicts
* open them one by one and look for lines starting with `<<<<<<HEAD`, these are the conflicting parts
* once the file is fixed up, `git add filename`
* once all of the files have been fixed, `git rebase --continue` to continue the rebase.
* more merge conflicts will likely pop up after continuing (it happens commit by commit), so you'll have to rinse and repeat this process a couple of times.

`lasagne.layers.dnn` is not imported by default, you need to do:
```python
import lasagne.layers.dnn
```
The reason is that it can only be imported on machines with GPU and cuDNN. If we auto-import it on machines that have cuDNN and silently not import it on machines that don't, there's no place we can throw a meaningful error message if somebody tries to use `Conv2DDNNLayer` on a machine that doesn't support it. So we require importing it explicitly. We should probably add a hint to the top of http://lasagne.readthedocs.org/en/latest/modules/layers/dnn.html to make users aware of that. Disassembling the binary that I posted before it is possible to see that at **0xA40** there is a call to the symbol **__arm_ioremap**.
The problem is that the target of the call in angr is wrong (basically the target address is 4 bytes more than the correct one and this happen for every call):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3390748/32022828-63d075cc-b98c-11e7-82a3-b2c9760e5bea.png)

I read the [documentation of ARM about ELF](http://infocenter.arm.com/help/topic/com.arm.doc.ihi0044f/IHI0044F_aaelf.pdf) and I might figured out what the problem is.
As of now the formula to compute the target address in angr is:
```result = ((S + A | T) - P``` (as described in Table 4-9 of the document).
The problem is that the operation pointed out in Table 4-12 (and the note above it) for B and BL is missing.

Implementing (my understanding of) that operation, the formula would become like this:
```result = ((S + (A << 2) | T) - P```

Using the new formula the target of the jump is correct:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3390748/32023580-839e929c-b98e-11e7-8826-1f2dcc2ae192.png)

**BONUS**: The **result** must be in **AND** with **0x03FFFFFE** instead of **0x03FFFFFC** as it is now

Hey, so I have only recently come in contact with this issue, but I support fish in saying that I am against blowing away Path. Instead I would propose a clearer distinction between states and Paths. Here is an excerpt of things that I jotted down during a bout of insomnia last night. 

Take this with a grain of salt, it's only points I jotted down so I would remember it once I woke back up ;)

```
TL;DR: Move states into angr, make paths logically be the path how to get to a particular state, make Simuvex an engine. 
Process-state related to but not equal to environment-state.


1. Make sure all the information that is actually path information is tracked in the path!

 - no more constraints, log, previous_run, etc. In the states!
 - easier deduplication of multiple states
 - possible to split paths on memory write ( this is currently handled solely in simuvex, 
   we could make it possible for a path to specify which it wants and why)      
 - paths without states, states without paths possible ( possible to prune states of paths 
   we don't care about any more while keeping trace, guards, etc.) (States without paths as 
   it's currently possible of course)


2. Rather than merging angr and simuvex, make angr use and expose all the simuvex magic 
and coolness in the paths! ( PATH-INSPECT-BREAKPOINTS, path solving, actions, log, events, guards, etc. )


3. States can never be unsatisfiable! They can only be unreachable. Keeping states seperate 
and having the constraints in the paths makes this clear. I realized this from fish's constant 
corrections about the difference between ASTs and Constraints which I incorrectly kept using 
interchangeably. States have ASTs, paths have constraints! This distinction is important.


4. Move engines to paths => hit JNI? SOOT engine => VEX engine.
Similar to paths, set initial engine during initialization, everything else happens based on 
triggers, e.g. path breakpoint can switch engine, switch state, set satisfiability, 
introduce constraints, etc.


5. Move hooks and inspect-breakpoints to paths. That way angr-gdb is gonna be way cooler! Set the 
hooks on the initial path, when something special happens => remove them and install different hook

That way we also get rid of the dependence on simuvex for breakpoints. Breakpoints are neat and cool 
=> they should be available for other stuff

Imagine someone writes a bf engine that also exposes instruction, mem_read and mem_write hooks => boom 
you get tracking that for free if you hook 'mem_read'. Oh, you wanted only simuvex memory reads? Hook 
'simuvex.mem_read instead' (engine names?)


6. Simprocedures to paths => simply different engine, it's free! Engines would have to be changed 
slightly to return successor states as well as additional path changes, e.g added constraints for 
each successor, etc.


5 + 6 solves weirdness of passing simuvex.SimProcedure to angr.Project for a hook? The project as I 
understand it doesn't necessarily have to do with execution. Have it on the path instead! Paths are 
made to track and perform execution


6. Have engine information stored on the path: unicorn concretization limits, vex simplification level, 
how to lift for this engine (bytes from binary vs. Bytes from state, vex from bytes, vex from program, 
only operates on bytes directly (brainfuck engine for example)), etc.

vex special function python implementations (ccalls or whatever they are called) maybe too?


Possibly:

    loaded library engine => base_addr + the mapping of addr to summary simprocedure
    syscall engine => syscall num to simprocedure mapping, 

Both simply calling the real simprocedure engine internally, or they can also be part of the 
simprocedure engine


The really cool thing: This in combination with a cool lifter pipeline ( see my BB changes ) allows us to do 
stuff like 

 - load bytes from program until a path has detected a jump to written memory => switch to loading bytes from state
 - Only decode SOOT => JNI call => decode vex for simuvex => return from JNI => back to decoding SOOT


7. States are the state of the process not the state of the environment. 

Process state: registers, memory, held locks, open file descriptors, open network connections, current MM I/O mapping, etc. => on State!
Environment state: file contents, network communications, I/O interactions, loaded libraries, etc. => on path

8. Paths with multiple states: one java state, one native state
```

j = Job()

I think you are missing the point of having Redis here.

We have three backends, Permissions, Cache and Storage.

The reason we have implemented the three backends with Postgresql and Redis is to prevent user from having to install both if they want to.

You are right that using Redis for the storage backend in production has got some drawbacks but using Postgresql for cache and permission is not a good fit either.

Having both is also the guarantee that the interface exposes can work with both NoSQL and SQL databases so that we can use any backends.

Most of kinto clients will never use filtering or sorting (kinto.js for instance).

You are true that we should enable transaction for the Redis storage backend and we will work on it eventually but postgresql backend didn't had transaction either at first.

The reason why we have both is not because Alex and Rémy are fans of Redis it is because Redis is currently our best option for the permission and cache backend.

Seems this is related to the library in use:
https://bugs.python.org/issue9334

The issue is caused by your PUT command, and running it in bash / most terminals.

`"Test$Test"` converts to `"Test${Test}"`, so it looks up the value of `Test` which is probably nothing, thus it stores only `"Test"`

In terminals like Bash, you can disable variable interpolation in strings by using `'`  instead of `"`.

eg,

```sh
credstash -t TABLE_NAME put keyname 'Test$Test'
```

The wsgiref could be solved by using [environment markers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0496/) in `requirements.txt`, so that `pip` ignores the package if `python_version` does not match. Something like this:
```
argparse==1.2.1
pyee==0.1.0
wsgiref==0.1.2 ; python_version == '2.7'
```
The downside is that the `pip` version on i.e. Ubuntu 15.10 is a bit older, so it doesn't understand markers; once you create a virtualenv, before running `pip install -r requirements.txt`, one should do `pip install --upgrade pip`. If this limitation is acceptable, I could do a small fix so that `requirements.txt` works for both Python 2 and 3.

I will try to address this properly in code, but two specific points-

> So again, the encodings are standardized, and what every implementation can support is up to them, and might change depending on the platform.

mike42/escpos-php#62 is a counter-example for the expectation that code pages are consistently implemented. In any case, legacy code pages are very niche, so I think we will need to be prepared to bridge the gap between what the computer speaks, and what the printer understands.

I used to see a lot of bug report traffic from Windows users who were struggling to encode. An encoding cache is now bundled with the escpos-php library, so `iconv` is no longer a run-time dependency. I think this driver could eventually introduce something similar if it becomes a problem.

> What good is it to list the code pages at all? Relying on the platform's encoding names works for 90% of the use cases

There are different responsibilities between the profiles (which map numbers to code pages on the printer), and the actual code page data (which map UTF-8 to the code page characters). So by storing our own list, we have a single source of truth for how each code page can be generated, keeping the profiles as simple as possible.

Also, based on what I've typed out so far, it does not appear safe to assume that `"".encode()` will be sufficient on its own (there is no lowest-common-denominator for printer code page support). See also [encoding.yml](https://github.com/mike42/escpos-snippets/blob/553eb569af50d552ad04f994144eb1ffd5f3c3c6/capability-db/data/encoding.yml)
I'm open to any of the above mentioned licenses for my contributions to this project.

@soggysec I tested this issue using the following minimal cmd2 application:

```Python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import cmd2

class OutputTrap(object):
    def __init__(self, handle):
        self.stdout = handle.stdout

    def write(self, msg):
        self.stdout.write(msg.rstrip() + '\n')

    def flush(self):
        pass

    def read(self):
        return 'stdin trap!'

class TestApp(cmd2.Cmd):
    def __init__(self):
        cmd2.Cmd.__init__(self)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = TestApp()
    app.stdout = OutputTrap(app)
    app.cmdloop()
```

I verified that this issue is present on both Python 2.7 and 3.x.  

However, I don't see a clear way to alleviate the underlying issue which would make sense in general for all use cases.  The underlying problem is that Python's subprocess module expects a legitimate file object (or at least file-like) if you pass it a stdout in the subprocess.Popen() call.  Your OutputTrap class isn't a legitimate file-like object.

Possible solutions I can see include:

1.  Replace **stdout=self.stdout** with **stdout=sys.stdout**
    - Advantage:  Simple
    - Disadvantage:  Output always goes to screen and is never redirected
2.  Replace **stdout=self.stdout** with **stdout=subprocess.PIPE**
    - Advantage:  Simple (just need to remember to get the output from calling communicate)
    - Disadvantage:  Many shell commands behave completely differently when their stdout is a pipe versus a tty
3.  Make your ``OutputTrap`` class more file-like so subprocess.Popen() will behave correctly
    - Advantage:  Don't have to change cmd2
    - Disadvantage:  Potentially more work on your part
4.  Override the ``do_shell`` method in your application to behave like you would like it to
    - Advantage:  Don't have to change cmd2 and easier than option 3 above
    - Disadvantage: Not a general solution

So what are you thoughts?  I'm leaning towards not trying to "fix" cmd2 for this issue because it does appear to be behaving as intended and I can't think of a way to work around this which would make sense in general for all use cases.  Without knowing more about what you want to do, I think that Option 4 above probably seems like the best path forward for you.  You can effectively choose to implement either Option 1 or Option 2 above for your particular case by overriding the ``do_shell`` method in your application.

Inheriting directly from Python classes like that isn't feasible: method availability isn't determined until runtime in Python, but needs to be known during compilation in C++.

What you'll need to do instead is write a C++ class that provides the methods you want and maps these into Python function calls.  So, for example, this class could store a `py::object file_;` containing the Python `file` object, then have (for example) a `std::string readline() { return file_.attr("readline")(); }` and similar methods for whatever other parts of the API you want to expose.

Hi @Christian-Remboldt,

> pi@raspberrypi:~ $ sudo ./certbot-auto --apache certonly -d chriscri5jes2p4z.onion

Let's Encrypt only issues "Domain Validated" (DV) certificates because this is the only type that can be issued automatically at web scale. The [baseline requirements](https://cabforum.org/baseline-requirements-documents/) that all publicly trusted CAs have to obey specifically forbids issuing for any `.onion` domains unless the certificate is an "Extended Validation" (EV) certificate. Since Let's Encrypt can not issue EV certs automatically we can't issue for `.onion` domains.

This means for the time being it's impossible to use Let's Encrypt to issue a certificate for `chriscri5jes2p4z.onion`. I'm going to close this issue since it isn't something Certbot can fix.

If you're interested in more information I recommend reading @schoen's [recent email](https://cabforum.org/pipermail/public/2017-November/012451.html) to the CA/Browser Forum mailing list. It summarizes the current problems & what might be done in the future to help make this possible.

Hope that helps,

@rimas-kudelis, yes it is possible to work around this issue with a `renew-hook`. Such a script might look something like:

```
#!/bin/sh
for domain in $RENEWED_DOMAINS; do
    if [ "$domain" = example.org ]; then
        cat "$RENEWED_LINEAGE/privkey.pem" "$RENEWED_LINEAGE/fullchain.pem" > /desired/path/to/server.pem
        service lighttpd reload
    fi  
done
```

To use this script with Certbot, you'd change your renewal command to something like `certbot renew --quiet --renew-hook /path/to/above/script.sh`. You can include other `if/elif` branches if you want to perform other actions when renewing a different certificate.

<VirtualHost *:80>

This is actually a duplicate of #6172

It's almost certainly related. In general, the problem is that Certbot sometimes attempts to log information while processing and post-processing its arguments, however, the logging handlers cannot be setup until we've processed the arguments that affect logging settings.

There are at least three solutions to the problem:
1. Stop logging during argument processing/post-processing
2. Move setting up logging to be the first thing that happens during post-argument processing.
3. Have a simple argument parser that just parses the logging arguments. After this parser has run, we can set up logging and then do normal argument parsing.

I personally don't think 1 is a good idea. There's actually lot of work that goes on while processing/post-processing our arguments and losing logging info there would be unfortunate. An argument could be made that this work should be moved elsewhere which might be a good idea in the long term, however, I don't think this is necessary to fix such a simple problem.

I don't have a strong preference between 2 and 3. 3 is more robust, but also more elaborate and more work. Post-processing happens [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/cli.py#L375) and logging setup is [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/main.py#L739). Main argument parsing begins [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/main.py#L729), however, if we went with approach 3, we could probably move the logging setup before the `plugins = plugins_disco...` line.

If you're interested in working on this, we'd certainly accept a PR. If you have any more questions, feel free to comment on this issue or join us in `#letsencrypt-dev` on Freenode.

to define two queues for the same host. The first one is for small tasks running on the head node (e.g. `rsync`), and the second one is for real large tasks. Submitting large jobs to the first one (and crash the head node) will get me grounded but this works for me nicely now.

So what you are proposing can be done in two ways,

1. define `host1_test`, `host1_1` or something with a `max_running_hosts` set to 1, then define `host1` as the regular full service queue.
2. allow the config definition to use some sort of command line argument like `-q host1 jobs=1`.

If using for Arrival_distribution:

The first time to be sampled is `0` (as the simulation begins at `t=0` and `time_dependent_function(0)` is `0`).

If sampling a `0` for inter-arrival times the simulation will get stuck in an infinite loop:
At time `0` a customer arrives, then after `0` time units another customer arrives, then after `0` time units another customer arrives, and so on. Hence getting stuck in an infinite loop and the time not incrementing.

Could the function be adapted so that an inter-arrival time of `0` is not sampled?
It's nasty. Due to it's unpredictable nature, it makes you slowly distrust every piece of software on the system.

- This is an experimental API. It is intended to get experience with the functionality and eventually develop a high level API.

Maybe this helps someone:

0. close all your notebooks and your jupyterlab server
1. You need ipympl (= matplotlib/jupyter-matplotlib)
2. Be clean in your installed versions. The way I clean up when things are messed up:
  a. conda remove ipympl
  b. pip uninstall ipympl (in case I mixed GH/pip installs with conda)
  c. repeat b. until no ipympl is found, there can be more than one!
3. conda install ipympl
4. perform the required post-conda install steps as advised per their readme:
  a. `conda install nodejs`
  b. `jupyter labextension install @jupyter-widgets/jupyterlab-manager`
5. When you launch jupyterlab and it asks to build stuff, do it! ;)

If you already have installed `nodejs` you can of course skip 4a.
Also, there may be other steps you can skip, but this procedure helped me to get my matplotlib figures back, using `%matplotlib ipympl`

@njsmith Using the same runtime syntax for both "ordinary" programming and metaprogramming has consequences. The payoff is that you make it easier for people to take the step up to doing their own metaprogramming. The cost is that you lose the ability for the *compiler* to readily warn about certain things - it doesn't mean IDEs can't warn about it (especially beginner oriented ones like Mu that can make different assumptions from those the reference interpreter makes).

And if an instructor's reaction to that recommendation is "But I want to teach absolute beginners to program with a plain text editor, not an IDE", then they need to go read http://blog.osteele.com/posts/2004/11/ides and ask themselves some serious questions about whether or not that approach is really in the best interests of their prospective students.

Whatever API we come up with needs to have some provision for doing per-run setup/teardown kind of stuff that happens outside of the cancel-scope-under-test. For example, you might want to fire up an HTTP server, start a client against it, and then cancel the server but *not* the client, and then make some assertions about what the client sees. And you need a new client each time.

> Does it make any sense for trio to support micropython?

Thanks for considering that, but I don't think it's practical. There're 2 main reasons:

1. It won't work on MicroPython, as that (deliberately) lacks various "convenience" things.
2. It won't work on "microcontroller", or rather, you'll have a hard time finding one where it will. Because it's *very* heavy. (So, the only realistic port to run it on would be "Unix" port).

In general, MicroPython "zen" is described here: https://github.com/micropython/micropython/wiki/ContributorGuidelines


But at the same time, we criticized asyncio with almost the same words (https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/32#issuecomment-347330239), while approaching it from different size:

* Myself, from the criteria of minimality and minimal resource usage
* You, from the criteria of correctness, efficiency, and robustness.

So, we can compare to what we came in the end ;-). Also, if you had an idea how to do something in Trio (very) simply, but that turned out to be too simple for its intended usage, well, that could be just good for uasyncio. Or if you would look into uasyncio and find something blatantly incorrect or which can be simplified beyond what it already is, I'll gladly accept accept criticism. I'd also appreciate just discussion, but from the above, it should be clear that my focus is on minimality (more specifically, how to do as much as possible with as little as possible, finding a balance there), so I'm not sure if you're interested in that.

Oh, I'll be glad to share any criticism of Trio too, as I'm going to learn from it ;-).
